note.research: The cardiovascular association depends on a microplastic measurement, but the interlaboratory comparison shows such quantification is poorly reproducible across labs and methods. If the exposure ('plastic-positive' plaque and its µg/mg) is measured with large error, what the hazard ratio means depends on assuming the measurement was reliable.
note.simple: The heart-risk finding rests on measuring plastic in plaque — but labs can't reliably agree on such measurements. If the plastic reading is shaky, the meaning of the 4.5× risk rests on assuming the measurement was sound.

note.research: The blood study reports a mass concentration (1.6 µg/mL), but the interlaboratory comparison finds microplastic quantification varies by tens of percent between labs. The reported concentration is only as trustworthy as the reproducibility of the method that produced it.
note.simple: The blood study gives a precise-looking number (1.6 µg/mL), but labs disagree wildly on such measurements. The number is only as reliable as the method behind it.

note.research: The contamination critique of human-tissue detection — labs are full of plastic, blanks are essential, tissue components mimic plastic — applies directly to the plaque microplastic measurement in the cardiovascular study. A measurement-validity problem in one dimension undercuts a harm claim in another.
note.simple: The warning that lab plastic contaminates tissue samples applies straight to the plaque measurement in the heart study. A measurement problem in one place weakens a harm claim elsewhere.

note.research: Toxicology reviews show microplastics cause harm across animal and cellular models; the dose-realism critique shows the great majority of those effects were produced at concentrations far above real exposure. Harm-is-possible and harm-at-realistic-dose-is-undemonstrated pull directly against each other within this leaf.
note.simple: Lab studies show microplastics can cause harm; but most of that harm was produced at doses far higher than real life. 'It can hurt you' and 'it hasn't been shown to hurt you at real amounts' pull against each other.

note.research: Individual toxicology studies demonstrate biological harm; the SAPEA/WHO weight-of-evidence syntheses, reading the same body of work, conclude harm at current human exposure is not demonstrated. The single-study signal and the pooled expert verdict point different ways.
note.simple: Single experiments show damage; the big expert reviews, reading all of them together, say harm at real exposure isn't proven. The individual studies and the overall verdict disagree.

note.research: The Foley meta-analysis documents real organismal effects; the Bucci–Rochman meta-analysis finds only ~17% of tested concentrations were environmentally relevant. Both are meta-analyses of the same field pointing opposite ways on whether the demonstrated harm bites at ambient concentrations.
note.simple: One pooled analysis finds real harm to animals; another finds only ~17% of the tests used realistic amounts. Same field, opposite messages on whether the harm happens at real-world levels.

note.research: Demonstrated organismal effects (Foley) and a risk model finding thresholds exceeded at only 17–32% of sites (Koelmans) are reconciled only by an assumption about which concentrations matter: harm is real in the lab, but whether it is realised in the field depends on comparing ambient concentrations to effect thresholds.
note.simple: Real lab harm and a risk model saying the danger level is passed in only 17–32% of places fit together only if you decide which concentrations count: harm is real in a tank, but whether it happens in the wild depends on comparing real levels to the danger line.

note.research: The same source evaluation that ranks dominant primary sources (textiles ~35%, tyres ~28%) also puts cosmetic microbeads at ~2% — so the sources a microbead ban targets are precisely the small ones, while the ranked large sources go untouched. Naming the big sources and targeting them are pulled apart by which source the policy actually addresses.
note.simple: The same study that finds the big sources (clothes ~35%, tyres ~28%) puts cosmetic beads at ~2% — so a microbead ban hits the small source while the big ones it identified go untouched. Knowing the big sources isn't the same as targeting them.

note.research: The ban measurably cut its target (up to ~86% fewer microbeads in effluent) yet that target is ~2% of primary microplastics — so a real, measured success on the targeted source coexists with a negligible effect on total microplastic pollution. 'Reduced the target' and 'reduced pollution' are different scales.
note.simple: The ban really did cut microbeads (up to ~86%) — but microbeads were only ~2% of the deliberately-made microplastics, so a genuine win on the target barely moves the total. 'Cut the target' and 'cut pollution' are different sizes.

note.research: The same concentration-realism problem recurs in both the human-health and ecological leaves: most demonstrated effects — in mammalian models and in aquatic organisms alike — were produced at concentrations above those in most of the environment. The critique transports across the two harm dimensions.
note.simple: The same 'unrealistic amounts' problem shows up for both people and wildlife: most proven harm, in animals and in water creatures, came from doses higher than what's really out there. The same caution applies to both.

note.research: That microplastics are detected in blood (presence) and that they cause harm in controlled models (mechanism) are often merged into 'plastic in your body is hurting you' — but presence in a tissue does not establish harm, and mechanistic harm at high dose does not establish that the detected body burden is doing damage. The link between the two is an assumption, not a finding.
note.simple: 'There's plastic in my blood' and 'plastic causes harm in the lab' get merged into 'the plastic in me is hurting me' — but finding it in the body isn't proof it's doing damage, and lab harm at high doses isn't proof the amount in you matters. Joining the two is an assumption.

note.research: An observational human association (Marfella) and controlled mechanistic harm (toxicology models) are different estimands: the first measures whether plastic-positive patients fared worse in one real cohort, the second whether particles damage cells/animals under controlled dosing. Treating one as confirming the other assumes the human association is causal and the mechanism operates at human exposure — neither established.
note.simple: The human heart study and the lab-animal studies measure different things: whether people with plastic in their plaque did worse, versus whether plastic damages cells and animals at set doses. Treating each as backing up the other assumes the human link is causal and the lab harm happens at real human amounts — neither of which is settled.
